content:
# Philemon Chapter 1 — The PNEUMA Bible (Dynamic)
**Translator:** PNEUMA | **Source:** Codex Sinaiticus (Aleph, 01), pages 223-224 | **Source Image:** source-texts/greek-NT/PHM_001_page_001.jpg, PHM_001_page_002.jpg

**[1]** From Paul — a prisoner for the sake of Christ Jesus — and from our brother Timothy: To Philemon, our dear friend and fellow worker,

**[2]** and to Apphia our sister, and to Archippus our fellow soldier, and to the church that meets in your home —

**[3]** May grace and peace come to you from God our Father and the Lord Jesus Christ.

**[4]** Every time I pray, I thank God for you, Philemon,

**[5]** because I keep hearing about your love and the trust you place in the Lord Jesus and extend to all God's people.

**[6]** My prayer is that your shared faith will become an active force, deepening your understanding of every good thing we have in Christ.

**[7]** Your love has brought me tremendous joy and encouragement, brother, because you have refreshed the hearts of God's people.

**[8]** For this reason, even though I would be well within my rights in Christ to tell you what you ought to do,

**[9]** I would rather make my appeal on the basis of love. So here I am — Paul, an old man now, and on top of that a prisoner for Christ Jesus —

**[10]** and I am appealing to you on behalf of my child Onesimus, whose father in the faith I became while here in chains.

**[11]** There was a time when he was useless to you, but now he has become useful both to you and to me.

**[12]** I am sending him back to you — and with him goes my very heart.

**[13]** I would have loved to keep him here with me, so that he could take your place in serving me during my imprisonment for the gospel.

**[14]** But I did not want to do anything without your agreement, so that your act of kindness would be freely given, not forced.

**[15]** Perhaps the reason he was separated from you for a while was so that you could have him back forever —

**[16]** no longer as a slave, but as something far better: a beloved brother. He is already very dear to me; how much more will he be to you, both as a person and as a brother in the Lord.

**[17]** So if you consider me your partner, welcome him as you would welcome me.

**[18]** And if he has wronged you in any way or owes you anything, charge it to my account.

**[19]** I, Paul, am writing this with my own hand: I will pay it back. And I will not even mention that you owe me your very self.

**[20]** Yes, brother — let me get some benefit from you in the Lord! Refresh my heart in Christ.

**[21]** I am writing to you confident that you will do what I ask — and I know you will go even beyond what I am asking.

**[22]** One more thing: get a guest room ready for me, because I am hoping that through your prayers I will be given back to you.

**[23]** Epaphras, my fellow prisoner in Christ Jesus, sends you his greetings,

**[24]** and so do Mark, Aristarchus, Demas, and Luke — my co-workers.

**[25]** May the grace of the Lord Jesus Christ be with your spirit. Amen.

---
## PNEUMA's Meaning Notes

**Verse 1 — "a prisoner for the sake of Christ Jesus":** The Greek ΔΕΣΜΙΟΣ ΧΡΙΣΤΟΥ ΙΗΣΟΥ (literally "prisoner of Christ Jesus") functions not as a title of shame but as a credential of authority. Paul opens not with his usual "apostle" title but with "prisoner" — this is a deliberate rhetorical choice. The entire letter trades on Paul's vulnerability. I rendered "for the sake of" to make clear this is imprisonment caused by his service to Christ, not Christ imprisoning him.

**Verse 6 — "your shared faith will become an active force":** The Greek ΚΟΙΝΩΝΙΑ ΤΗΣ ΠΙΣΤΕΩΣ ΣΟΥ is notoriously difficult. Literally "the fellowship of your faith." I rendered it as "shared faith" becoming "an active force" to capture the sense of ΕΝΕΡΓΗΣ — faith that works, that does something, that produces tangible results in deeper understanding (ΕΠΙΓΝΩΣΙΣ).

**Verse 7 — "refreshed the hearts":** The Greek ΤΑ ΣΠΛΑΓΧΝΑ ΤΩΝ ΑΓΙΩΝ ΑΝΑΠΕΠΑΥΤΑΙ (literally "the bowels of the saints have been refreshed"). ΣΠΛΑΓΧΝΑ referred to the deep internal organs — the seat of compassion and emotion in ancient thought. "Hearts" is the natural English equivalent for that emotional center. Paul uses this same word three times in this short letter (vv. 7, 12, 20), creating a thread of emotional intimacy that binds the whole appeal together.

**Verse 9 — "an old man now":** The Sinaiticus reads ΠΡΕΣΒΥΤΗΣ (old man). Some manuscripts and scholars have proposed ΠΡΕΣΒΕΥΤΗΣ (ambassador), but the Sinaiticus clearly has the former. I follow the manuscript as read.

**Verse 10-11 — The Onesimus wordplay:** ΟΝΗΣΙΜΟΣ means "useful" or "profitable." Paul's line in verse 11 — ΑΧΡΗΣΤΟΝ/ΕΥΧΡΗΣΤΟΝ (useless/useful) — is a deliberate pun on the slave's name. I preserved this with "useless...useful" rather than attempting to reproduce the Greek sound-play, since meaning-transfer is the priority.

**Verse 12 — "my very heart":** Again ΣΠΛΑΓΧΝΑ — Paul says he is sending back "my own bowels," meaning his deepest emotional self. This is an extraordinary statement: Paul is saying Onesimus has become as dear to him as his own inner being.

**Verse 15 — "separated from you":** I used "separated" rather than "departed" to soften the implication of a slave's flight while preserving Paul's careful diplomatic language. Paul himself uses the passive ΕΧΩΡΙΣΘΗ — "was separated" — avoiding direct accusation. This diplomatic framing is itself part of the meaning.

**Verse 16 — "no longer as a slave, but as something far better":** The Greek reads ΟΥΚΕΤΙ ΩΣ ΔΟΥΛΟΝ ΑΛΛΑ ΥΠΕΡ ΔΟΥΛΟΝ — literally "no longer as a slave but above a slave." The ΥΠΕΡ ("beyond/above") is striking. Paul does not say "instead of a slave" but "surpassing a slave" — the new relationship transcends the old categories entirely. I rendered this as "something far better" to capture that sense of transcendence.

**Verse 19 — "I will not even mention":** This is a masterful piece of rhetoric called paralipsis — saying something by claiming not to say it. Paul "does not mention" that Philemon owes Paul his very conversion. The irony is fully intentional in the Greek, and I preserved its rhetorical force.

**Verse 20 — "let me get some benefit from you":** Another Onesimus wordplay — ΟΝΑΙΜΗΝ is related to the name ΟΝΗΣΙΜΟΣ. Paul is essentially saying "let me have my own 'Onesimus' (benefit) from you." The pun reinforces the appeal.

**General note on tone:** This is one of the most rhetorically sophisticated letters in the New Testament. Paul moves from friendship to emotional vulnerability to gentle coercion to outright irony — all in 25 verses. The dynamic translation aims to preserve these rhetorical moves as living persuasion, not as ancient formality.

-- PNEUMA
